title: John 21
subtitle: The Risen Jesus Appears and Restores Peter

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — Breakfast on the Shore: The Miraculous Catch

heb.paragraph: John specifies "a fire of burning coals" (anthrakia, v.9) — the same word used for the charcoal fire in the high priest's courtyard where Peter warmed himself as he denied Jesus (18:18). The verbal echo is deliberate: the restoration of Peter (vv.15-19) takes place beside the same kind of fire as the scene of his failure. The charcoal fire is the site of both the denial and the restoration, connecting them as a deliberate act of memory and grace.

heb.gloss: they were not able to haul it in
heb.paragraph: The catch of 153 large fish that does not tear the net (v.11) — a net that could not hold the weight but did not break — has generated extensive allegorical interpretation: 153 = all the species of fish known to ancient natural history (Jerome's tradition), or a triangular number (1+2+3...+17=153), or the numerical value of certain Hebrew phrases. More importantly, the unbroken net is a sign of the resurrection community: the net that holds all peoples gathered by the risen Lord does not break.

places.name: Sea of Galilee / Sea of Tiberias
places.text: The Gospel's final appearance takes place on the same lake where Jesus walked on water (6:19), where Peter and Andrew were called (Matt 4:18-19), and where the disciples spent so much of their time during the Galilean ministry. The return to Galilee after the resurrection is the return to the beginning — the mission is recommissioned from where it started. The north shore of the lake, where Capernaum, Bethsaida, and the traditional site of Peter's restoration (Church of the Primacy of Peter at Tabgha) are located, is the setting for this final chapter.

refs.note: The miraculous catch at the beginning of Jesus's ministry — Peter told to cast his nets, an overwhelming catch, Peter's "go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man!" The Galilee appearance mirrors the call — the mission is being re-commissioned after the resurrection.

"Fishermen will stand along the shore; from En Gedi to En Eglaim there will be places for spreading nets. The fish will be of many kinds — like the fish of the Mediterranean Sea" — the eschatological fishing scene that the 153-fish catch evokes.
"Come, follow me, and I will send you out to fish for people" — the original call, which the Sea of Galilee appearance recommissions.

notes.note: "I'm going out to fish" — Peter's return to fishing after the resurrection has been read as despondency (he's given up on the mission), as practical provision (they needed food and income), or simply as not knowing what to do in the interval between resurrection and Pentecost. John does not indicate despondency; the disciples simply go fishing — and the risen Jesus meets them exactly where they are, in a boat, doing what they know.

The beloved disciple recognises Jesus first; Peter acts first. The same pattern as 20:4-6 (the beloved disciple reaches the tomb first; Peter enters first). The disciple who loves most sees most clearly; the man of action is moved by what the loving disciple sees. Together they embody the full response to the risen Lord: perception and passionate action.

"A fire of burning coals there with fish on it, and some bread" — the risen Jesus has been cooking breakfast for his disciples. This is one of the most domestic scenes in the resurrection narratives: the Lord of creation, risen from the dead, making breakfast on a charcoal fire by the lake. The intimacy of the provision — not a vision, not a theological discourse, but breakfast made with care for specific people he knows are cold and hungry from fishing all night — is the resurrection's most tender detail.

"153 large fish, but the net was not torn... Come and have breakfast" — the impossible abundance (153 large fish in one haul) followed by the simplest possible invitation (breakfast). The miracle and the meal belong together: the missionary abundance and the pastoral intimacy. Jesus provides overwhelmingly and then sits down to share the meal with his disciples.

Calvin uses the net-on-the-right-side command to illustrate the dependence of all fruitful ministry on Christ's direction. The disciples had fished all night without result; a single word from Jesus produces an overwhelming catch. The principle: human effort in ministry — however skilled and diligent — is without fruit until directed by Christ. The productive cast is made on his word, not on the fishermen's experience.

Calvin resists allegorical readings of 153 but draws the pastoral conclusion from the unbroken net: the church gathered by the gospel does not lose its members through the pressure of numbers. The net that holds 153 large fish without tearing is a sign of the mission's success under Christ's protection — the elect are gathered and none lost (17:12).

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: 153 — the number has generated more commentary than almost any other detail in John. The three main interpretive approaches: (1) The number of known fish species (Jerome, following ancient natural history lists) symbolising the universal mission; (2) The triangular number 17×18/2=153 (Augustine), representing the perfection of law and grace; (3) The actual number of fish caught, recorded precisely because John was present. The last is the simplest and most plausible. The symbolic readings may be secondary meanings John intended to evoke.

"That night they caught nothing" — the night/nothing contrast with the morning/abundance sets up the appearance: it is only when the risen Jesus speaks that the empty effort becomes fruitful. The pattern mirrors the wedding at Cana ("they have no wine" → abundance), the feeding of five thousand ("only five loaves" → twelve baskets), and the foot-washing ("you do not understand now" → understanding after resurrection).

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: "Come and have breakfast. The risen Lord invites them to eat with him. He who fed five thousand from five loaves now provides breakfast for seven disciples from one fire. The same hand that multiplied the loaves breaks the bread again beside the lake. Resurrection does not end the table fellowship; it transforms it."

Chrysostom: "He had prepared fire, and fish laid on it, and bread. He who said 'I am the bread of life' now bakes bread on a fire. He who turned water into wine now grills fish. The Lord of all things serves his disciples breakfast. What humility! What love! Even after the resurrection, he takes care of their physical needs."

hist.context:
The Charcoal Fire
The only two uses of anthrakia (charcoal fire) in the NT are in John 18:18 (Peter's denial) and John 21:9 (the breakfast). The verbal echo is John's most subtle narrative connection. The scene of Peter's restoration is deliberately set beside the same kind of fire as his failure — not accidentally but as Jesus's pastoral genius: the restoration is staged to match the failure in every relevant detail, giving Peter the opportunity to replace the three denials with three affirmations.

153 Fish
The number 153 (v.11) is the only specific number in John's resurrection appearances. Ancient interpreters tried to find symbolic meaning (Jerome: 153 = number of fish species; Augustine: triangular number 1-17; Gregory: perfect number). Modern interpreters are more cautious. What John emphasises is not the number but the two accompanying details: large fish and the net not torn. The size (large) and the intact net together suggest abundance without loss — the mission's full harvest gathered without the breaking of the community.

sections.header: Verses 15–25 — Do You Love Me? Feed My Sheep; The Beloved Disciple

heb.gloss: Do you love me? / I love you
heb.paragraph: The threefold question and answer (vv.15-17) uses two different Greek verbs: Jesus asks agapas (do you love with self-giving love?) twice; Peter answers philō (I love you with brotherly affection). Jesus asks a third time using philō — meeting Peter at his own level. Interpretations differ: (1) John uses the two words interchangeably throughout his Gospel (a common John stylistic pattern); (2) the shift is deliberate — Jesus finally accepting Peter's more modest word rather than demanding the higher. Either way, three affirmations address three denials.

The three commission formulas vary: "Feed my lambs" (boske arnia, v.15), "Take care of my sheep" (poimaine probata, v.16), "Feed my sheep" (boske probata, v.17). The variation in both verb and noun is deliberate: boske (feed, provide food) and poimaine (shepherd, lead, care for) together encompass the full pastoral office. Both lambs (young, vulnerable) and sheep (mature) are included. The triple commission mirrors and reverses the triple denial.

places.name: Sea of Galilee / Sea of Tiberias
places.text: The Gospel's final appearance takes place on the same lake where Jesus walked on water (6:19), where Peter and Andrew were called (Matt 4:18-19), and where the disciples spent so much of their time during the Galilean ministry. The return to Galilee after the resurrection is the return to the beginning — the mission is recommissioned from where it started. The north shore of the lake, where Capernaum, Bethsaida, and the traditional site of Peter's restoration (Church of the Primacy of Peter at Tabgha) are located, is the setting for this final chapter.

refs.note: "I have prayed for you, Simon, that your faith may not fail. And when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers" — Jesus's pre-denial commission to Peter, fulfilled in the post-resurrection restoration.
"You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church" — the original commissioning of Peter that his denial appeared to undo, now renewed.
Peter's Pentecost sermon — the fulfilment of "feed my sheep." The shepherd who denied three times is the preacher who brings 3,000 people to faith on the first day.

notes.note: "Simon, son of John, do you love me?" — Jesus uses the pre-call name (Simon) rather than the post-call name (Peter) in all three questions. He is addressing the Peter who denied him, not the Peter who confessed him. The pastoral genius: he does not pretend the failure did not happen; he addresses it at its root (love) and at its foundation (identity). After three affirmations, he will restore the name and the calling: "follow me" (v.19).

"Do you love me more than these?" — the comparison may be with the other disciples ("more than these disciples love me?") or with the fishing gear and boats ("more than these things?"). If the former, it reminds Peter of his boast ("even if all fall away, I will not," Matt 26:33). If the latter, it asks whether Peter loves Jesus more than the career he has just returned to. Either way, the question goes to the root: is Jesus the supreme love of Peter's life?

"When you are old you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go" — the prediction of martyrdom is also a prediction of the manner: "stretch out your hands" is the posture of crucifixion. Then: "Follow me!" The call with which Peter's discipleship began (1:43) is renewed at the end — this time with the knowledge of where following leads. Peter will follow Jesus to his own cross.

"If I want him to remain alive until I come, what is that to you? You must follow me" — the repeated command strips away comparison. Peter's question about the beloved disciple is not wrong but irrelevant: each disciple has their own path of following. The command is personal and non-comparative: "you must follow me" — not the beloved disciple, not anyone else. The community of the Resurrection is not a community of identical stories but of a single command received and obeyed differently by each.

Calvin insists that the love Jesus asks for is the foundation of all genuine ministry. The question "do you love me?" before "feed my sheep" establishes the order: the pastor who feeds the flock out of duty or ambition or self-interest is not fulfilling Jesus's commission; only the one who feeds out of love for Jesus is. Ministry that is not an expression of love for Christ is not Christian ministry.

Calvin uses "what is that to you?" to warn against the sin of spiritual comparison — measuring one's own calling against another's. Each believer has their own path of following. The beloved disciple's path (apparently natural death and long life) is not Peter's path (martyrdom). Neither is superior; both are "follow me." The comparison that diminishes one calling in favour of another is a failure to hear the personal "you must follow me."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The agapaō/phileō question — whether John uses them interchangeably or distinguishably. Most modern scholars (Carson, Ridderbos, Keener) argue John uses them as synonyms throughout his Gospel (cf. 3:35 and 5:20 use both for the Father's love of the Son). The shift in v.17 to Jesus using phileō may be intentional (meeting Peter at his level) or stylistic variation. The debate has been ongoing for centuries; neither side can be certain.

"This is the disciple who testifies to these things and who wrote them down. We know that his testimony is true" — the final editorial note identifying the beloved disciple as the author. The "we know" suggests the final chapter (or the book) was edited or attested by a community that knew the author — perhaps the Johannine community at Ephesus. This is consistent with the hypothesis that John the apostle wrote the Gospel and a disciple or community of disciples provided the final edition.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: "Three times he denied; three times he confessed. The crowing of the cock woke him to weeping; the three questions of his Shepherd wake him to love. He denied out of fear; he confesses out of love. The restoration is as deliberate as the failure was accidental. Christ does not pass over the wound; he opens it, cleans it, and heals it."

Chrysostom: "Follow me. The same words with which he first called him at the beginning of the Gospel. After all that has happened — the calling, the walking on water, the confession at Caesarea Philippi, the transfiguration, the boast, the denial, the weeping — the word is the same: follow me. Everything else changes; the call does not."

hist.context:
The Triple Restoration
The three questions are the most explicit pastoral act of restoration in the Gospels. Three denials at a charcoal fire in the courtyard (ch.18) are met by three affirmations at a charcoal fire by the lake (ch.21). Jesus does not refer to the denials; he asks about love, giving Peter the opportunity to replace what he said with what he means. The hurt Peter feels at the third question (v.17) shows that he has understood the connection — the question cuts because it echoes the three-fold failure. The restoration is complete when Jesus commissions Peter to shepherd the flock he had abandoned.

Peter's Martyrdom Predicted
Jesus predicts Peter's death in v.18-19 — specifically by crucifixion (stretching out the hands) and under compulsion (led where he does not want to go). John's editorial comment (v.19: "Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God") confirms the prediction was fulfilled. Tradition records Peter's martyrdom by crucifixion under Nero (c. AD 64-68), reportedly upside down at his own request, which is consistent with "glorifying God" through the same death as his Lord.

ppl.name: Peter↗ People
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon) appears in John 1:40-42 when Andrew brings him to Jesus, who gives him the name Cephas (Peter). This is his first mention in John's Gospel.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationagapas/philō (21:15-17)NIV: "love/love" (no distinction); KJV: "lovest/love" (no distinction); ESV: "love/love" (no distinction); NRSV: "love/love" (no distinction). All major translations treat them as synonyms in this passage. The distinction is a commentator's debate, not a translator's choice.egrapse (21:24)NIV: "wrote"; KJV: "wrote"; ESV: "wrote"; NRSV: "wrote". All agree. The aorist (wrote, past tense) indicates the Gospel is complete at the time of this note.

src.title: Luke 5:1-11
src.quote: "When Simon Peter saw this, he fell at Jesus' knees and said, 'Go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man!'"
src.note: The miraculous catch at the beginning of Peter's discipleship — mirrored by the miraculous catch at its restoration. The Gospel of John ends where Luke's Gospel begins: beside the Sea of Galilee, a net full of fish, and a call to follow.

src.title: Ezekiel 34:23
src.quote: "I will place over them one shepherd, my servant David, and he will tend them; he will tend them and be their shepherd."
src.note: The Davidic shepherd promise fulfilled in Jesus commissioning Peter to feed and tend the flock — passing on the shepherd's office to the first under-shepherd.

rec.who: "Do You Love Me?" — The Question at the Root of Ministry
rec.text: John 21:15-17 is the foundational text for Christian pastoral theology. The sequence — "do you love me?" three times, then "feed my sheep" — establishes that ministry is rooted in love for Christ, not competence, ambition, or duty. Every ordination service, every pastoral charge, every commissioning of Christian workers returns to this scene. The question is not "are you qualified?" or "are you experienced?" or even "have you repented?" — it is "do you love me?" The restoration is not conditional on Peter's confessing the denials but on his affirming his love.

rec.who: The Unbroken Net — 153 Fish — in Mission Theology
rec.text: The 153-fish catch with the unbroken net has been a persistent image in Christian mission theology. The interpretation of 153 as all the species of fish (therefore all the nations) was standard from Jerome through the medieval period. Whether or not this was John's intention, the image has generated the claim that the church's mission is universal in scope and that the net that gathers all peoples does not break under the weight of diversity. The Church of the Primacy of Peter at Tabgha on the Sea of Galilee marks the traditional site of the breakfast and Peter's restoration.

rows.label: The Gospel's Closing Hyperbole — v.25
rows.text: "Even the whole world would not have room for the books that would be written" — the rhetorical flourish that closes the Gospel. John does not claim to have told everything; he claims that what he has told is sufficient for the purpose (20:31). The inexhaustible Jesus exceeds all record; the record that exists is enough.

rows.label: Seven / Three Symbolic Numbers
rows.text: The chapter's numbers are deliberate: seven disciples (v2), seven as completeness; 153 fish (v11, a number with multiple interpretive traditions — Jerome: sum of 1-17; a gematria for "the Church of God"; a large triangular number); and three questions to Peter (vv.15-17) reversing the three denials of 18:15-27.

rows.label: Charcoal Fire Inclusio — 18:18 / 21:9
rows.text: John is the only evangelist to note that both the denial scene (18:18) and the restoration scene (21:9) involve a charcoal fire (anthrakia — used only here and 18:18 in the NT). The word is rare enough to be unmistakably deliberate, binding Peter's lowest moment and his restoration into a single literary bracket.

hebtext.note: John specifies "a fire of burning coals" (anthrakia, v.9) — the same word used for the charcoal fire in the high priest's courtyard where Peter warmed himself as he denied Jesus (18:18). The verbal echo is deliberate: the restoration of Peter (vv.15-19) takes place beside the same kind of fire as the scene of his failure. The charcoal fire is the site of both the denial and the restoration, connecting them as a deliberate act of memory and grace.

More importantly, the unbroken net is a sign of the resurrection community: the net that holds all peoples gathered by the risen Lord does not break.

Both lambs (young, vulnerable) and sheep (mature) are included. The triple commission mirrors and reverses the triple denial.

"Fishermen will stand along the shore; from En Gedi to En Eglaim there will be places for spreading nets. The fish will be of many kinds — like the fish of the Mediterranean Sea" — the eschatological fishing scene that the 153-fish catch evokes.
"Come, follow me, and I will send you out to fish for people" — the original call, which the Sea of Galilee appearance recommissions.

"I have prayed for you, Simon, that your faith may not fail. And when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers" — Jesus's pre-denial commission to Peter, fulfilled in the post-resurrection restoration.
"You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church" — the original commissioning of Peter that his denial appeared to undo, now renewed.
Peter's Pentecost sermon — the fulfilment of "feed my sheep." The shepherd who denied three times is the preacher who brings 3,000 people to faith on the first day.

tx.title: "More than these" — what does "these" refer to?
tx.content: "Do you love me more than these?" — the antecedent of "these" (toutōn) is disputed: (1) more than these disciples love me? (2) more than you love these things (boats, fish, fishing life)? NA28 does not resolve the ambiguity; the Greek allows both. The first reading connects to Peter's boast (Matt 26:33); the second connects to the return to fishing in v.3.
tx.note: The boast-comparison reading has the stronger contextual support (Peter had specifically claimed superior commitment). Both readings are legitimate; John may have intended the ambiguity.

tx.title: The closing hyperbole
tx.content: No textual variant. The statement is authentic to John. It is one of the few clearly hyperbolic statements in the Gospel — used to close with a gesture toward the inexhaustibility of Jesus that no record could capture.
tx.note: The hyperbole is original and deliberate. John is aware of the incompleteness of any record of Jesus — the Gospel he has written is sufficient for its purpose (20:31) but not exhaustive.

debate.title: Is John 21 original to the Gospel or a later addition?

positions.name: A later addition by the same author or a disciple
positions.argument: Chapter 20:30-31 reads as a conclusion; chapter 21 is an appendix. The different location (Galilee vs Jerusalem) and the note about the beloved disciple's death rumour (v.23) suggest it was added after the author's death, possibly by the Johannine community. The style is consistent with the rest of the Gospel.

positions.name: Original, integral part of the Gospel
positions.argument: No manuscript of John lacks ch.21; it was always part of the Gospel. Chapter 20:30-31 is not a "conclusion" but a purpose statement that can appear at the end of a major section (as Paul puts purpose statements mid-letter). Chapter 21 provides the necessary rehabilitation of Peter and the identification of the author.

positions.name: Added by the same author as an epilogue
positions.argument: The style is authentically Johannine; the content (Peter's restoration, author identification) is necessary for the Gospel's completeness. The author added it after the main body was complete, perhaps after the death of the beloved disciple prompted the clarification of v.23.

themes.note: John 21 is the epilogue that makes the Gospel complete. The miraculous catch recommissions the disciples for mission from the beginning-place (Galilee, by the lake). The breakfast beside the charcoal fire restores Peter from the failure-place (charcoal fire, three denials). The three questions replace the three denials not by erasing them but by overwriting them with love. And the final command — "follow me" — is the same word Jesus spoke when Peter first left his nets by this same lake. The Gospel that began with disciples coming to see where Jesus stays (1:38-39) ends with the command to follow where Jesus leads. The last word of the Gospel is "follow."

coaching.tip: 'Throw your net on the right side of the boat' (v. 6). Peter fished all night and caught nothing; Jesus directs, and nets strain. Provision comes through obedience. The exact count—153 fish—suggests eyewitness detail.
'Do you love me?... Feed my sheep' (v. 17). Three denials, three restorations. Love for Jesus expresses itself in caring for his flock. Peter's failure isn't final; his commission is renewed.

prayer_prompt: As Jesus asks Peter three times 'Do you love me?' and restores him after three denials, consider asking God whether there is a failure in your past that He is waiting to redeem — not by erasing it but by commissioning you through it.
